description: Seeds are raised in wet nursery beds with standing water. Maintain shallow water layer.
Seedlings prepare for transplanting. Ensure adequate nursery nutrition.
Active tiller formation. Requires continuous irrigation. Apply urea and weed field.
Reproductive stage begins. Critical for yield. Apply potassium to strengthen stems.
Critical water dependency. Protect from stem borers and fungal infections. Avoid raining.
Grains start to harden and turn yellow. Maintain soil moisture but avoid flooding.
Drain water 10 days before harvest. Cut the stems close to the ground when grains are golden.
Proper layout and spacing are essential. Plant healthy suckers in well-prepared, manured pits.
Rapid leaf production. High demand for nitrogen and potassium. Regularly remove weeds and extra suckers.
The inflorescence (bunch) emerges. Provide strong physical props/supports to prevent plants from toppling under weight.
Fingers start swelling rapidly. Apply the final dose of potassium. Bag the bunches if pest pressure is high.
Fingers lose their sharp angles and become rounded. Harvest while still green for transport.
Plant stem cuttings near living standard trees. Ensure shading from direct sun and maintain high humidity.
Vines rapidly climb the support. Prune to encourage lateral fruiting branches. Apply organic cow dung.
Tiny white flowers appear on spikes. Rain or high humidity is absolutely critical for successful pollination.
Berries fill out on the spike. Protect crops from the devastating quick wilt disease by improving field drainage.
Spikes are ready to pluck when one or two berries turn noticeably red or yellow. Sun-dry immediately.
Propagate from robust clonal cuttings. Maintain under controlled shade nets with regular misting.
Careful centering and tipping are required. The goal is to develop a wide, spreading bush frame rather than vertical height.
Bush reaches plucking height. Establish a flat plucking surface to maximize leaf generation.
Pluck tender shoots consisting of "two leaves and a bud" at intervals of 7-14 days depending on growth flush.
Plant robust crowns or suckers in very well-drained sandy loam. Basal fertilizer application is important.
Plant forms a dense rosette of spiny leaves. Keep weed-free; the shallow root system is easily outcompeted.
Natural flowering occurs, but can be synchronized by spraying ethylene-based chemicals to organize harvest.
The composite fruit slowly swells and gains weight. Ensure adequate soil moisture but strictly zero waterlogging.
Harvest when the base of the fruit turns distinctly yellow-orange and emits a sweet fragrance.
Germinate seed nuts under heavy shade. Ensure the soil remains moist but completely prevents water stagnation.
Palms grow vertically, discarding older fronds. Protect the spindle (growing point) from spindle bug attacks.
Spathe opens to reveal flower clusters. Ensure robust irrigation to prevent the shedding of button-sized immature nuts.
Prevent fungal Koleroga disease with Bordeaux mixture. Harvest bunches when the husks shift to a bright yellow-orange.
Plant selected seed nuts horizontally or vertically in shaded raised nursery beds. Keep consistently irrigated.
Sapling develops split leaves. Apply organic matter like coir pith into the planting basin to retain summer moisture.
Inflorescence opens year-round. Consistent moisture is legally required to avoid massive button (immature nut) shedding.
Nuts take ~11 months to fully mature. Harvest every 45-60 days. Monitor strictly for rhinoceros beetle damage.
Plant healthy mother or finger rhizomes in raised beds. Apply a thick layer of mulch (like green leaves) instantly.
Rapid leaf growth. Control weeds manually. Top dress with nitrogen and apply a second round of mulch.
Foliage growth stops. Energy funnels down into massive rhizome expansion. Prevent water stagnation at all costs.
Leaves begin turning completely yellow and drying out. Carefully dig out the rhizomes without bruising them.
Plant 20cm stem cuttings vertically or tilted in loose, well-draining mounds. Sprouting occurs quickly.
The bush develops a dense leafy canopy. High requirement for potassium fertilizer to initiate early tuber formation.
Tubers rapidly expand underground. Ensure the soil remains loose to allow unhindered tuber growth. Avoid heavy watering.
Harvest when basal leaves turn yellow and drop off. Lift the entire plant gently to avoid snapping the brittle tubers.
Plant budded stumps in standard pits. Protect the delicate bud patch from direct hot sunlight using bamboo baskets.
Maintain a pristine, weed-free basin. Apply complex fertilizers. Ensure a single, straight, unbranched main trunk up to 3 meters.
Side branches develop. Apply fungicides via fogging or spraying to prevent the devastating abnormal leaf fall disease.
Commence latex tapping strictly when the trunk girth reaches 50cm at a height of 125cm from the bud union.
Plant treated rhizome bits with visible buds facing upwards. Heavy mulching is absolutely essential to protect against heat.
Multiple leafy shoots emerge. Earth up the soil around the base to encourage rhizome spread. Ensure proper drainage.
Rhizomes bulk up significantly. Control shoot borers. Yellowing of lower leaves is normal during this late phase.
Harvest when the foliage turns pale yellow and naturally begins to wither and dry. Clean rhizomes thoroughly.
Plant epicotyl grafts in heavily shaded areas. Very sensitive to direct sunlight; construct artificial shade huts if necessary.
Develops a dense, conical canopy. Prune dead or heavily shaded inner branches. Highly responsive to organic manure grids.
If using seedlings, ensure at least a 1:10 ratio of male to female trees for vital pollination. Fruits take 9 months to mature.
Do not pluck. Harvest only when the yellow fruit splits entirely open, revealing the highly prized striking red mace inside.
Transplant robust seedlings at the onset of monsoons. Direct sunlight is lethal to young plants; provide thatched shade.
Growth is notoriously slow. Deep, infrequent summer watering is required. Apply slow-release NPK in a circular trench.
Clusters of buds appear at the tips of branches. Protect foliage from fungal leaf spots which can destroy the yield.
Timing is critical. Hand-pick the flower buds exactly when their base transitions from green to a distinct pink flush, before opening.
Prune the center of the canopy to allow extreme sunlight penetration. Apply the main bulk of organic and chemical fertilizers now.
Strictly withhold all irrigation to induce heavy flowering. Spray preemptively against devastating mango hopper and powdery mildew.
Fruits swell. Provide light irrigation to prevent premature fruit drop. Tie bags around high-value fruits to stop fruit flies.
Pluck fruits selectively with an 8mm stalk still attached to prevent sap burn on the skin. Harvest when shoulders outgrow the stem.
Plant seedlings on raised mounds in extremely well-drained soil. Even 24 hours of waterlogging will rot the roots and kill the plant.
Grows rapidly with a hollow trunk. Highly demanding of potassium and boron. Apply micronutrient foliar sprays.
Remove any male plants (unless keeping 10% for pollination) and violently eradicate any plants showing crinkled mosaic virus leaves.
Harvest carefully when a distinct yellow streak breaks out on the apical end of the green fruit skin.
Plant healthy tillers under dense forest canopy. This crop mandates 50-60% permanent shade and a thick layer of natural leaf litter.
Tillers multiply into a large clump. Fork the soil lightly around the base and apply split dosages of NPK fertilizer. Avoid deep digging.
Panicles shoot out at ground level. Active honeybee populations are biologically required for pollination. Keep the ground clear of debris.
Capsules mature sequentially. Harvest manually every 30 days, picking only the firm, mature capsules while they are still dark green.
Plant seedlings in well-drained pits. Ensure adequate shading during the first year to prevent serious sunburn to the delicate leaves.
Coppice (cut back) the main stem to a height of 15cm to force the explosive growth of 5-6 straight, healthy lateral side shoots.
Shoots mature. The ideal time for harvest is determined by peeling a tiny test strip; if the bark separates easily, the sap flow is perfect.
Cut the straight shoots. Scrape off the rough outer bark and expertly peel the inner bark, rolling it into standard quills for drying.
Plant heavily shaded seedlings, ideally as an intercrop between towering Arecanut or Coconut palms. Protect from heavy wind.
Crucial phase: Prune to establish a strict "jorquette" (branching point) at 1.5 meters height. Remove low-hanging chupon water shoots.
Tiny flowers appear directly on the main trunk and heavy branches (cauliflory). Ensure thick leaf litter to breed pollinating midges.
Pods swell and completely change color. Harvest only fully mature yellow/orange pods by cutting the stalk cleanly without damaging the trunk cushion.
Transplant polybag seedlings carefully to avoid taproot bending. Maintain under standard shade trees (like silver oak).
Perform strict centering and desuckering. Establish a strong, wide primary framework of branches to maximize future berry yield.
Rain (or sprinkler irrigation) triggers synchronized, massive white flowering. Protect against the devastating coffee berry borer.
Berries transition to a deep crimson red. Hand-pick only the fully ripe "cherries" selectively over multiple rounds for premium quality.
Plant meter-long cuttings near support trees (like Glyricidia). Gently tie the growing vine to the standard and bury the basal nodes in thick humus.
When the vine reaches 2 meters, cleanly loop it back down to the ground. Induce mild moisture stress to shock the plant into producing flower buds.
Flowers open early in the morning and only last one day. Immediate, skilled manual hand-pollination is required using a small stick or needle.
Beans elongate. Harvest precisely when the outermost tip turns pale yellow. Immediately begin the complex sweating and curing process.
Plant softwood grafts deeply in standard pits. Highly drought-tolerant but immensely benefits from summer irrigation during its first year.
Remove all sprouts arising from the rootstock below the graft union. Prune crisscrossing branches to allow deep sunlight penetration.
The tree puts out brilliant red new flushes followed by flowers. Aggressively spray to protect against the lethal tea mosquito bug.
The swollen cashew apple drops to the ground with the true nut attached. Collect fallen nuts daily, detach the apple, and sun-dry the raw nuts.
Plant healthy, 3-budded setts end-to-end in deep furrows. Irrigate immediately. Germination completes rapidly within three weeks.
Formative stage requires immense nitrogen. "Earth up" the soil around the base to anchor the rapidly lengthening, heavy canes against lodging (falling over).
Vegetative growth ceases. Sugar brutally accumulates in the internodes. Withhold massive irrigation during the final month to aggressively spike sucrose levels.
Canes turn yellowish and metallic. Harvest manually by cutting absolutely flush with the ground, as the deepest basal internodes hold the highest sugar concentration.
